new_prompt: Listen to the story: Today Emma turned five years old. Her mom baked a big chocolate cake for the party. Emma was so happy. Why did Emma's mom make a chocolate cake?
Listen to the story: Bear felt very hungry. First he looked up in a tall tree for honey. Then he ate the sweet honey. What did Bear do right after he felt hungry?
Listen to the story: Frog wanted to reach the other side of the pond. So he hopped onto a green lily pad. It carried him across the water. Why did Frog get on the lily pad?
Listen to the story: At lunchtime, Mia opened her bag. Her lunch was not there. She had left it at home. Her kind friend Leo shared his food with her. What was Mia's problem in the story?

Listen to the story: Turtle and Rabbit ran a race. Rabbit ran far ahead and stopped to take a long nap. Slow Turtle kept walking and walking. Turtle passed Rabbit and won. How did Turtle win the race?
Listen to the story: Miss Lee made a new book. She thought hard and wrote all the words on the pages. That made her the author. What does an AUTHOR do?

Listen to the story: Mom poured water into a tray and froze it. The water turned into hard, cold ice. Later the ice got warm and melted back into water. What does the passage help us understand about how water and ice are connected?

Listen to the story: A fact book about bugs was made by two people. One person wrote all the true facts about bugs. The other drew clear pictures to help you understand them. Why do informational books need BOTH an author AND an illustrator?

Listen to the story: We read two books about cats. Book 1 told us that cats like to sleep a lot. Book 2 told us that cats like to chase toys. What is one way the books tell us different things about cats?

Listen to the story: It was group story time. Miss Kim read a book to the class. The best listeners kept their eyes on the pictures and stayed quiet. What is one way to show you are actively listening during group story time?
Listen to the story: Sam read a story, but some parts got too tricky. He did not give up or quit. He raised his hand and asked his teacher for help. What should you do when a story gets tricky?

Listen to the story: The class read a story together. Ben listened closely and understood it well. He raised his hand and asked a good question about what happened. What is one way you can show you understand a story during group reading time?

Listen to the story: Miss Ray read a story to the whole class. As she read, she asked questions about it. The engaged children raised their hands and answered them. How can you show you are actively engaged when your teacher is reading a story to the whole class?

Listen to the story: Our group read a fact book about the sea. It had interesting pictures of fish and whales. We talked about the pictures together and learned new things. When your group reads a fact book, what is a good way to learn new things together?
Listen to the story: A kitten climbed up a tall tree. It went very high and got stuck. It could not get back down, so it cried and cried. Why was the kitten scared?

Listen to the story: Leo could not ride his bike. He fell down again and again, but he did not give up. He kept trying, and soon he could ride all by himself. What lesson can we learn from Leo's story?

Listen to the story: An old woman lived in a small house. Her little house sat right next to the ocean. Every morning she watched the waves and the boats. Which words best describe where the old woman lives?
Listen to the story: First, Rosa found a lost puppy in the grass. Then she gave it some water. At last, she found the puppy's owner. Which major event began the story about Rosa?
Listen to the story: Mia read a storybook. It had bright pictures and was very long. It was about a talking dragon that flew to the moon, all made up and not real. Which is TRUE about storybooks?

Listen to the story: Sam read an informational book about real sharks. It taught true facts, like how sharks swim and eat. Nothing in it was make-believe, and it was not silly or funny. Which is TRUE about informational books?

Listen to the story: Real seeds cannot talk or yawn like people. In real life, a seed just needs water and sun to grow. Only in a make-believe story can a seed do things like a person. Which sentence sounds like it is from a make-believe story?

Listen to the story: A story book has make-believe characters who do things, like a bear who goes to school with his friends. A fact book does not tell a tale; it just shows real things, like a list of animal names or real bug photos. Which book tells a STORY?

Listen to the story: An information book gives you true facts about real things, like the sun. Make-believe books tell tales that are not real, like a magic hat, a flying cat, or a talking fish. Which book gives real INFORMATION?

Listen to the story: In one story, a hungry bear looks all around the woods to find honey to eat. In another story, a busy bee flies from flower to flower and works hard to make honey. What is different about how the bear and the bee experience honey in their stories?

Listen to the story: A short book tells us just one fact about cats. It says that cats like to sleep a lot, sometimes all day. The book says nothing about their tails, chasing mice, or climbing trees. Which detail about cats is shared in the text?

Listen to the story: The book tells about George Washington. First, he led the army in a big war and helped win it. After the war was over, people chose him to be the first president. How does the text show the connection between George Washington leading the army and being president?

Listen to the story: Ben wanted to know what page Chapter 3 started on. The table of contents at the front of his book lists each chapter and the page it starts on. The glossary and index at the back only list words and topics, and the cover just shows the title. Where would you look to find what page a chapter starts on?

Listen to the story: Each part of Mia's book had a heading in big letters at the top. The heading told what that section was about, like 'Baby Animals.' It did not say who wrote the book, how many pages were left, or when it was made. What does a heading at the top of a page tell you?

Listen to the story: In Sam's book, the words told about a bird. A picture cannot make sounds or smells, and feelings are told by words. But the picture showed the exact colors and shapes of the bird, its bright red feathers and round body. What can pictures in a book show that words cannot?

Listen to the story: In her book, the author says kids should eat their vegetables. She gives one reason: vegetables have vitamins that help you grow strong. She does not say they taste sweet, and she never talks about sharing food or growing taller. What reason does the author give to show why kids should eat vegetables?

Listen to the story: Leo wanted to play fetch with his dog, Biscuit. He looked all over the yard for a stick but could not find one, so he grabbed the fuzzy yellow tennis ball from his backpack instead. He threw it far, and Biscuit raced after it and brought it right back. What object did Leo use to play fetch with Biscuit?

Listen to the story: Grandpa and Tomas worked all morning to build a little wooden birdhouse. Before they began, they had already painted the boards a bright blue color. As soon as the very last nail was hammered in, they carried the birdhouse outside and hung it on a tree in the backyard. What did Grandpa and Tomas do right after they finished building the birdhouse?

Listen to the story: Ava could not wait to play in her big soccer game on Saturday. But when she woke up that morning, dark clouds filled the sky and heavy rain poured down. Her coach called to say the field was too wet, so the game could not happen. Ava felt very disappointed. Why was Ava disappointed?

Listen to the story: George Washington Carver was a scientist who loved studying peanuts. He worked hard to find hundreds of new ways to use them. From peanuts, he made useful things for people, such as soap and paint that families could use in their homes. Which of these items did George Washington Carver create using peanuts?

Listen to the story: To make sea salt, workers first fill shallow pools with salty ocean water. Then they wait for hot, sunny days. The warm sun heats the water until it slowly disappears into the air, and only the salt is left behind in the bottom of the pool. When making sea salt, why is it important that the sun heats the ocean water?

Listen to the story: Maya opened a nonfiction book about the ocean. At the top of each part, she saw big, bold words like "Ocean Animals" and "Ocean Plants." Before she even started reading, those words told her exactly what each part of the book would be about. What is the purpose of a heading in a nonfiction text?

Listen to the story: In her book about animals around the world, the author added a large, colorful map. On the map, she placed small pictures of animals on top of the different countries where they are found. By looking at it, readers could see which animals lived in each part of the world. Why would an author include a map in a book about animals around the world?

Listen to the story: The author of the article wanted kids to drink more water every day. She explained that water helps your body in many important ways. Most of all, she said, drinking water helps your brain work better so you can think clearly and learn new things. Which of these is a reason the author gives for why you should drink more water?

Listen to the story: In class, Maya's teacher explained that literal language means exactly what the words say, while figurative language paints a picture with words that are not meant to be taken word for word. The teacher wrote four sentences on the board and asked the students to find the one that was completely literal and could really happen just as it was written. Maya read each one carefully before raising her hand. Which sentence uses LITERAL language?

Listen to the story: The Amazon Rainforest is the largest rainforest on our planet, stretching across many countries in South America. Scientists have found that it produces about 20 percent of the world's oxygen, which is why some people call it the lungs of the Earth. It is also an amazing home to more than 10 million different types of plants and animals, many of which live nowhere else. Because so many living things depend on it, protecting the Amazon is very important. Which statement tells two important things about the Amazon Rainforest, according to the text?

Listen to the story: In 1955, in the city of Montgomery, a woman named Rosa Parks was riding home on a crowded bus. When the driver told her to give up her seat, she quietly refused, and she was arrested for it. The news of her brave choice spread quickly through the community. In response, thousands of people decided to stop riding the city buses altogether, and this boycott of the buses lasted for many months. What happened because Rosa Parks refused to give up her bus seat?

Listen to the story: Ben was reading a science article that described the layers of the Earth. The article explained that our planet has a hard outer crust, a thick middle layer called the mantle, and a very hot center called the core, all fitting inside one another like the layers of an onion. Ben wanted to add one picture that would help readers clearly see how these layers are arranged from the outside to the deep inside. Which illustration would BEST help a reader understand an article about the layers of the Earth?

Listen to the story: A rainbow is a curved band of colors that stretches across the sky, showing red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and purple in a bright arc. Rainbows appear when sunlight shines through tiny raindrops floating in the air. As the light passes into each drop, the drop bends it and splits it into all of its separate colors, and that is why we see the glowing arc after it rains. How do the first two paragraphs of the passage connect to each other?

Listen to the story: Priya was comparing two different articles about sharks for a class project. Her teacher reminded her that the smartest way to compare two texts is to notice which facts appear in both articles and which facts show up in only one of them. Priya sat down and thought carefully about the one question that would help her compare the texts the most. When comparing two texts, which question is MOST useful?

Listen to the story: Text A argues that children spend far too much time in front of screens, and it says that screen time should be limited so kids stay healthy and active. Text B agrees that screens can be overused, but it also points out that educational apps and learning shows can teach children useful new skills, so it says that educational screen time can actually be helpful. Both texts talk about screens, but they reach their points in different ways. Which statement best compares the main points of Text A and Text B?

Listen to the story: Maya wrote a play for her class. Part of her script read: MAYA (waving her arms): Wait for me! [She runs across the stage and trips over a chair.] Later, Maya rewrote the very same scene as a regular story: 'Maya waved her arms and shouted for her friends to wait. Then she ran and tripped over a chair.' In the play, the notes inside the brackets told the actors exactly how to move, but the regular story just described everything in plain sentences. Which structural element is found in a drama (play) but NOT in prose (a regular story)?

Listen to the story: Ben wrote the same conversation two ways. In his play, it looked like this: BEN: I found the treasure! ELLA: Show me! In his novel, the same talking looked like this: 'I found the treasure!' Ben shouted. 'Show me!' Ella replied. In the play, each speaker's name is printed right before the words they say. In the novel, quotation marks wrap around the spoken words instead. How is dialogue presented differently in a play versus a novel?

Listen to the story: Rosa's teacher showed the class a poem: 'The gentle breeze begins to blow, it lifts the kite and lets it go.' The words had a steady beat you could tap along to, and the ending words blow and go sounded alike. Then the teacher showed a regular story about a windy day, written in long sentences that flowed across the page with no steady beat and no matching end sounds. What structural element do poems often have that prose does not?

Listen to the story: In Sam's story, the narrator says, 'I was so nervous I could barely breathe, but I had no idea what my sister Kate was thinking as she stared out the window.' Because Sam wrote using the word I, readers only learn what the narrator himself sees and feels. We never find out Kate's private thoughts, only what the narrator can observe from the outside. How does first-person narration affect a story compared to third-person?

Listen to the story: One story begins like this: 'I walked down to the pond and dipped my toes in the cool water.' The narrator is a character inside the story, telling it by talking about themselves with one special word. Which word tells you a story is being told in first-person point of view?

Listen to the story: A teacher wrote three sentences on the board and asked her class to find the one where the writer is telling about themselves. She reminded them that first-person point of view uses the word I, which shows the narrator is a character in the story. Which sentence is told in first-person point of view?

Listen to the story: In the book, the narrator told readers, 'Deep down, Priya felt afraid, even though she smiled on the outside.' When Priya's class turned the book into a play, there was no narrator to share those private thoughts. The audience could only watch the actors and hear them speak, so Priya's hidden feelings were never told out loud. When a book is turned into a play, what might be LOST?

Listen to the story: When Jordan read the book, the words simply said, 'Grandpa was upset.' But when Jordan watched the movie of the same story, he saw the actor's deep frown, heard the shaky, angry sound of his voice, and noticed how he crossed his arms tightly. The movie let Jordan see and hear things that the plain words on the page could not show. What might a visual presentation (movie or play) ADD to a story that the text cannot?

Read the passage: A firsthand account is written by someone who was actually there and lived through the event themselves. A secondhand account is written by someone who studied or read about the event later but did not see it happen. For example, a diary a person wrote during a battle is firsthand, while a textbook written many years afterward is secondhand. Which is a firsthand account?

Read the passage: A firsthand account comes from a person who lived through an event, so it often shares their own feelings, senses, and personal experiences. A secondhand account comes from someone who learned about the event afterward, so it usually reports the facts in a calmer, more objective way, without personal emotion. How might a firsthand account DIFFER from a secondhand account of the same event?

Read the passage: A firsthand account lets you feel what one person actually experienced, sharing their personal perspective. A secondhand account gathers facts from many sources to give the bigger picture and broader context. When you read both kinds together, you understand an event more completely than you could from just one. Why is it valuable to read BOTH firsthand and secondhand accounts?

Read the passage: A firsthand account is told by a person who was there and experienced the event with their own senses. These accounts often use the word I and describe what the writer personally saw, heard, or felt at the moment it happened. Which sentence sounds like a FIRSTHAND account, where the writer saw the event with their own eyes?

Read the passage: A science book described the human digestive system in words, listing the stomach, the small intestine, and other organs one by one. But the sentences made it hard to picture how the organs fit together. Then the book added a labeled diagram. The diagram showed exactly where each organ sat and how they all connected in one long path, something that was very hard to imagine from the written description alone. How does a diagram of the human digestive system help a reader MORE than a written description alone?

Read the passage: When you read an author's argument, your job is to decide whether it is strong. A strong argument gives reasons and evidence that truly back up its main point. To judge it, a careful reader looks closely at each piece of evidence and asks whether it really supports the claim the author is trying to prove. When evaluating an author's argument, you should ask —

Read the passage: When researchers study a topic, they read many sources instead of just one. Each source may add a different perspective and new details, and one book might leave out something that another explains clearly. By combining several sources, a reader builds a more complete and balanced understanding of the topic than any single source could give. Why is using multiple sources better than using just one?
